decision: Collector fetches releases via GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases and returns RawItem with item_type='release'
reasoning: PROJECT_SPEC §5 V2 adds Releases; the pipeline downstream is item_type-agnostic so the work concentrates in the collector
rejected: GraphQL releases API (unnecessary; REST is sufficient and matches issue collection)

decision: Release identity is repo + 'release' + tag_name, reusing the state-store key contract
reasoning: state-store-3 keys on repo+item_type+item_id and is type-agnostic, so delta/idempotency work unchanged
rejected: numeric release id as item_id (renders as opaque #123456789 instead of #v1.2.0)

decision: RawItem.item_id = tag_name for releases
reasoning: tag_name is stable, unique-per-repo and human-readable so the digest line reads #v1.2.0; keeps the renderer unchanged
rejected: numeric release id

decision: Draft releases (published_at == null) are excluded; inclusion keyed on published_at
reasoning: a draft is not a published release; 'new releases' means published ones
rejected: include drafts (would surface unpublished notes)

decision: Prereleases are included
reasoning: a prerelease is still a release a contributor should see; easy to flip via the map filter if the operator disagrees
rejected: exclude prereleases

decision: RawItem.created_at = published_at (raw ISO string), used for the cutoff
reasoning: mirrors the issue mapping which stores the 'new' timestamp; published_at is the inclusion timestamp
rejected: created_at field of the release (differs from publish time)

decision: No digest-renderer delta — releases use the existing ### Release (N) group
reasoning: the living renderer already ships 'release' in GROUP_ORDER with the Release label (AC-5-006/AC-5-011); a MODIFIED delta would be a no-op and rejected by openspec validate, or would misrepresent existing behavior
rejected: add a digest-renderer MODIFIED delta as the kickoff task note suggested (renderer is already release-ready)

decision: fetch_releases is an adapter-only method; GitHubClient Protocol stays frozen; reuse CollectorConfig with no new tunable
reasoning: mirrors summarizer-llm-4 ADR-005 (batch helper on the adapter) and github-collector-2 ADR-001 (no new Config field); the constraint forbids new Config surface for adapter tunables
rejected: add fetch_releases to the GitHubClient Protocol
add a new Config/CollectorConfig field for releases

decision: No collector-side changelog truncation — reuse the summarizer's 8000-char input_char_cap
reasoning: summarizer-llm-4 prepare_input already caps title+body at input_char_cap before hashing and the LLM call, so a long release body is handled automatically
rejected: truncate the release body in the collector (duplicates the summarizer cap)

decision: pipeline._collect_all calls fetch_releases alongside fetch_items per repo and concatenates before delta/mark_seen
reasoning: pipeline.py is the only cross-stage importer (AC-7-002); wiring lives there, matching v2-001 delta placement
rejected: a separate release pipeline pass (would duplicate delta/summarize/render orchestration)

decision: Flag published-vs-created ordering skew (RISK-002) for the architect rather than resolving it at S1
reasoning: the /releases endpoint orders by created_at while inclusion keys on published_at; resolving early-stop vs full-page-filter is a design decision bounded by max_items_per_repo
rejected: silently keep created-desc early-stop (could drop a late-published old release)

decision: CONFIRMED: draft releases (published_at == null) are excluded
reasoning: user sign-off at SPEC-LOCK; a draft is not a published release
rejected: include drafts

decision: CONFIRMED: prereleases are included
reasoning: user sign-off at SPEC-LOCK; a prerelease is still a release the contributor should see
rejected: exclude prereleases

decision: CONFIRMED: RawItem.item_id = tag_name for releases
reasoning: user sign-off at SPEC-LOCK; stable, human-readable identity renders as #v1.2.0, renderer unchanged
rejected: numeric release id

decision: CONFIRMED: title = name, fallback to tag_name when null/empty
reasoning: user sign-off at SPEC-LOCK; guarantees a non-empty human-readable title
rejected: leave title empty when name is null

decision: CONFIRMED: created_at = published_at (raw ISO string)
reasoning: user sign-off at SPEC-LOCK; published_at is the inclusion timestamp, mirrors the issue mapping
rejected: use the release created_at field (differs from publish time)

decision: CONFIRMED: newest-first ordering with early-stop when published_at < cutoff; ordering-strategy tradeoff (RISK-002) deferred to architect S3
reasoning: user sign-off at SPEC-LOCK accepts early-stop; the accept-risk-vs-per-page-filter decision is an S3 design choice, not a spec ambiguity
rejected: resolve RISK-002 at S2 (it is a design-time tradeoff)

decision: CONFIRMED: per-repo release-fetch failure isolated (WARN + skip), never discards already-collected issues
reasoning: user sign-off at SPEC-LOCK; release fetch sits inside the same per-repo try/except as the issue fetch, preserving the v2-001 R1 partition-before-mark_seen invariant
rejected: let a release-fetch failure abort the whole repo (would lose collected issues)

decision: Early-stop pagination keys on created_at (the /releases endpoint's actual newest-first sort order) while inclusion keys on published_at (the requirement); mirrors fetch_items control flow one-to-one
reasoning: The endpoint returns releases created-desc and supports no sort=published param, so the 'everything after is older' early-stop guarantee only holds against created_at. Keying the stop on published_at (Option B) drops valid releases under created-desc ordering. Disabling early-stop entirely (Option C) contradicts the CONFIRMED AC-V2-003-013 and would be an S2-level scope change. The residual RISK-002 miss (created-before-window / published-within-window) is accepted as rare for a single-operator watchlist and pinned by a regression test.
rejected: Option B: early-stop on published_at (wrong under created-desc ordering)
Option C: disable early-stop, full-scan filter by published_at (contradicts CONFIRMED AC-013, unbounded pages)

decision: fetch_releases is an adapter-only method on GitHubCollector; the GitHubClient Protocol in ports.py stays frozen
reasoning: AC-V2-003-018 / BR-V2-003-003 mandate an adapter-only method; the pipeline holds a concrete GitHubCollector so it can call fetch_releases directly without widening the Protocol seam. Consistent with summarizer-llm-4 ADR-005 (batch helper on adapter, not Protocol).
rejected: Add fetch_releases to the GitHubClient Protocol (widens a frozen seam for a single concrete impl, violates AC-018)

decision: Both fetches run under one per-repo try/except; a narrow inner try/except wraps ONLY fetch_releases so a recoverable release failure yields releases=[] while issues already collected for that repo survive; a single _partition_new then a single mark_seen(items) run on issues+releases, preserving the v2-001 R1 partition-before-mark_seen invariant
reasoning: AC-V2-003-022 read literally requires that issues already collected for a repo (and all other repos) survive a release-fetch failure. A single try covering both fetches would lose the issues too if fetch_releases raises after fetch_items succeeds. AuthError and terminal RateLimitError are kept out of the inner catch tuple so fatal/partial-deliver semantics still reach the outer arms. One partition + one mark_seen keeps R1 intact.
rejected: Single try/except covering both fetches (loses issues when releases fail — violates AC-022)
Separate try per fetch with two mark_seen sites or deferred partition (risks partition-after-mark_seen R1 bug)

decision: No openapi.yaml is produced; the internal fetch_releases method contract is documented in design.md §API Design instead
reasoning: OSS Pulse is a CLI tool with no HTTP API — internal stage contracts are Python dataclasses. The design-rule/R5 'keep a SEPARATE openapi.yaml' is conditional ('if the change has an API') and not met. All 9 prior changes ship no openapi.yaml. Authoring one for the outbound-consumed GitHub call would misrepresent it as an exposed API.
rejected: Author an openapi.yaml describing the outbound GitHub /releases call (misrepresents a consumed API as an exposed one, pure ceremony, no consumer)

decision: _map_release falls back to str(id) as item_id when tag_name is missing but id exists; only returns None when BOTH are absent
reasoning: AC-V2-003-011 says skip when BOTH tag_name and id are missing; using str(id) as fallback preserves identity when tag_name is absent but the release can still be keyed
rejected: Return None whenever tag_name is missing (loses releases that have a numeric id)

decision: fetch_releases placed directly before fetch_items in client.py; dual-key loop: stop on created_at, include on published_at, skip draft with continue not return
reasoning: Mirrors fetch_items control flow exactly (ADR-001); draft continue-not-return is the spec-mandated behavior (AC-003); early-stop on created_at not published_at (ADR-001 tripwire)
rejected: stop on published_at (Option-B data-loss bug)
full-scan without early-stop (contradicts CONFIRMED AC-013)

decision: Inner guard catches (InvalidRepoError, NetworkError) explicitly plus CollectorError with isinstance re-raise for AuthError/RateLimitError, rather than listing only 3 specific subclasses
reasoning: AuthError and RateLimitError are subclasses of CollectorError. Catching CollectorError base in the inner guard would silently swallow AuthError (fatal) and RateLimitError (terminal partial-deliver). Using isinstance re-raise inside the CollectorError catch ensures any future CollectorError subclass other than Auth/RateLimit is treated as recoverable — consistent with the outer arm semantics.
rejected: Catch only (InvalidRepoError, NetworkError) — would miss future CollectorError subclasses
Catch (InvalidRepoError, NetworkError, CollectorError) without re-raise — swallows AuthError (confirmed by test)

decision: Added fetch_releases.return_value=[] stub to ALL existing pipeline tests and to test_cli_run.py::test_per_repo_outcome_log_emitted — 22 sites total — to fix TypeError from issues+MagicMock
reasoning: GitHubCollector is a MagicMock in all existing tests; after _collect_all changed to call fetch_releases, any test that doesn't stub it gets a MagicMock return, causing 'list + MagicMock = TypeError'. The stub approach is the mirror of how existing tests stub fetch_items — minimal, non-invasive, idiomatic.
rejected: Patch fetch_releases at pipeline module level (heavier, obscures the mock chain)

decision: ADR-001 stop key confirmed on created_at: test_risk002_regression_old_created_recent_published_is_missed PASSES (v1.9.9 NOT returned) — stop key was NOT reversed to published_at
reasoning: Tripwire test passes in QA independent run. The test asserts the ACCEPTED miss: release with created_at=30d ago and published_at=3d ago is skipped by early-stop. If the stop key were reversed to published_at, this test would FAIL (the release would be returned). Test passing = stop key is correct.

decision: R1 ordering confirmed: mark_seen receives issues+releases concatenated (count-invariant) — test_release_fetch_failure_issues_survive_other_repos_unaffected asserts mark_seen.assert_any_call([issue_a]) and assert_any_call([issue_b, rel_b])
reasoning: Code review confirms _collect_all concatenates items=issues+releases BEFORE _partition_new and mark_seen(items) — one call per repo with the full list. The count-invariant test directly asserts both calls with exact lists.

decision: Inner guard deviation confirmed correct: AuthError from fetch_releases propagates fatal — test_release_auth_error_not_swallowed_by_inner_guard PASSES; isinstance(exc,(AuthError,RateLimitError)) re-raise pattern is correct
reasoning: Code review confirms two-arm pattern: except(InvalidRepoError,NetworkError) absorbs; except CollectorError with isinstance re-raise ensures AuthError/RateLimitError escape the inner guard. Tripwire test verifies AuthError propagates as pytest.raises(AuthError).

decision: Documentation gap (non-blocking): proposal.md mentions README should gain a note about Releases, but no task was created and README was not updated
reasoning: The README note was a description in the 'What Changes' section of proposal.md, not a formal AC or task. It was deliberately not tracked. Functionality is unaffected. Recommended as follow-up in S6.
